The prophet, in a vision, sees the Messiah returning triumphantly from conquering his enemies, of whom Edom was an example. He travels, not exhausted from battle, but with great strength, prepared to overcome every opposing power. The Messiah declares that he had been treading the wine-press of the wrath of God; 19:13, and by his own power, without any human help, he crushed his stubborn opponents, because the day of vengeance was determined, being the appointed time for rescuing his church. Once, he appeared on earth seeming weak, to pour out his precious blood as an atonement for our sins; but he will in due time appear with great strength. The harvest ripens quickly; the day of vengeance, fixed and determined, approaches quickly; let sinners seek to be reconciled to their righteous Judge before he brings down their strength to the earth. Does Christ say, "I come quickly?" let our hearts reply, "Even so, come; let the year of the redeemed come."

The latter part of this chapter, and the whole of the next, seem to express the prayers of the Jews during their conversion. They acknowledge God's great mercies and favors to their nation. They confess their wickedness and hardness of heart; they entreat his forgiveness, and lament the miserable condition under which they have long suffered. The only-begotten Son of the Father became the Angel or Messenger of his love; thus he redeemed them with tenderness. Yet they murmured, and resisted his Holy Spirit, despising and persecuting his prophets, rejecting and crucifying the promised Messiah. All our comforts and hopes come from the loving-kindness of the Lord, and all our miseries and fears come from our sins. But he is the Savior, and when sinners seek after him, who in other ages glorified himself by saving and feeding his purchased flock, and leading them safely through dangers, and has given his Holy Spirit to prosper the labors of his ministers, there is good reason to hope they are discovering the way of peace.

They ask him to look down on the humble condition of their once-favored nation. Would it not be glorious to his name to remove the veil from their hearts, to return to the tribes of his inheritance? The Babylonian captivity, and the later deliverance of the Jews, were shadows of the events here foretold. The Lord looks down on us with tenderness and mercy. Spiritual judgments are more to be dreaded than any other calamities; and we should carefully avoid those sins which justly provoke the Lord to leave people to themselves and their deceiver. "Our Redeemer from everlasting" is your name; your people have always looked upon you as the God to whom they might appeal. The Lord will hear the prayers of those who belong to him, and deliver them from those not called by his name.
